sentence_reviews.text: Pairwise rankings or comparative judgments teach which outputs or behaviors are preferred.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Ouyang et al. RLHF paper, human comparisons/ranking model training; Rafailov et al. DPO paper, preference-pair objective setup; AIMA contents.html, decision/utility and learning chapters.
sentence_reviews.notes: RLHF and DPO support learning from comparative preferences; the sentence generalizes from output rankings to behaviors.

sentence_reviews.text: The chosen learning signal and interaction pattern may not match the deployment objective or data-generating process. Its direct dependencies include Supervised learning, Labels, targets, rewards & preferences.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: Synthesized from cited node sources plus the atlas dependency context in data/atlas.json.
sentence_reviews.notes: Limitation is reviewed as a broad synthesis/tradeoff statement for the family-level node, not as a direct quotation from one source.

sentence_reviews.text: Preference learning follows the learning setup expressed by Supervised learning.
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: RLHF paper supervised fine-tuning and supervised reward-model training from comparisons; DPO supervised preference loss over paired examples; AIMA Chapter 19 supervised learning.
sentence_reviews.notes: Preference learning often uses supervised datasets of comparisons; not every preference-learning method is purely supervised.

sentence_reviews.text: In this atlas graph, Preference learning is modeled as depending on Supervised learning, Labels, targets, rewards & preferences and feeding downstream nodes such as Margin, ranking & metric objectives, Reward modeling, Preference optimization (RLHF, DPO families).
sentence_reviews.evidence_location: data/atlas.json canonical node and dependency graph; generated from node dependencies and downstream consumers.
sentence_reviews.notes: This is an explicit internal atlas-graph positioning claim, not an external-world necessity claim.
